title: TA6: Using AI for research — LLMs in empirical finance
section: What LLMs are — and what they are not
text:
What LLMs are — and what they are not
A large language model is a statistical model trained to predict the next token in a sequence. It has no database, no memory between sessions (unless you give it one), and no ground truth. What it does have is an extraordinarily compressed representation of patterns in text — billions of human-written documents — which means it is extremely good at tasks that look like “what would a knowledgeable person write in response to this?”
This makes LLMs useful for:

Classification: is this 10-K’s risk-factor section positive, negative, or neutral?
Extraction: which named entities appear in this earnings call?
Summarisation: give me the main argument of this working paper in two sentences.
Generation: write a first draft of the related-literature section given these references.
Translation / normalisation: convert these inconsistently formatted firm names into a standard CUSIP-linkable key.

It makes LLMs unreliable for:

Factual lookup: “what was JPMorgan’s reported ROE in Q3 2023?” The model will give you a number. It may be wrong. Never use an LLM as a primary source for specific numerical facts.
Reproducibility over time: model providers update their models. A call with gpt-4o-mini today may return a different answer in six months even at temperature = 0. Cache your outputs.
Confidential data: calling an external API means your data leaves your machine. Never send proprietary, pre-publication, or personally-identifiable data to a commercial API without explicit clearance from your institution’s data governance and legal teams.


Setting up an OpenAI account
Three steps:

Go to platform.openai.com and create an account.
Navigate to API keys in your account dashboard and create a key (sk-...).
Set the key as an environment variable in R — never hard-code it in a script:

Sys.setenv(OPENAI_API_KEY = "sk-...")

# Or add it to your ~/.Renviron so it loads automatically in every R session:
# OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...

Verify the key is visible:

nzchar(Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))  # should return TRUE
Critical rule: your .Renviron file must be in your .gitignore. A leaked API key is a billing problem — attackers find leaked keys within minutes and use them to generate tokens at your expense.

title: TA6: Using AI for research — LLMs in empirical finance
TA6: Using AI for research — LLMs in empirical finance
text:
Prompt engineering
The quality of an LLM output is almost entirely determined by the quality of the prompt. Bad prompts produce bad outputs even with the best model; good prompts get useful results from cheap models.

The anatomy of a good prompt
Every prompt has two parts: the system message (sets the role and rules) and the user message (the specific input). For a classification task you want the system message to do four things:

Assign a role: “You are a financial analyst assistant.”
Specify the output format exactly: list every JSON field, its type, and its allowed values.
Set scope constraints: “Base your assessment ONLY on the provided text.” vs “Use your general knowledge.”
Eliminate ambiguity: if “neutral” means “no strong evidence either way”, say that explicitly.

Here is the system prompt we use for the excerpt-based analysis:
You are a financial analyst assistant. Analyze the provided Wikipedia excerpt about a bank and return a JSON object with EXACTLY these fields:
  sentiment       : one of 'positive', 'neutral', or 'negative'
  sentiment_reason: a single sentence explaining the sentiment
  business_lines  : an array of strings listing main activities mentioned
  summary         : a single sentence describing what the bank does
Base your assessment ONLY on the provided text. Do not use outside knowledge.

And the one for the general-knowledge reputation analysis:
  reputation_assessment: one of 'positive', 'neutral', or 'negative'
  key_controversies    : an array of 2-4 strings, each describing one issue
  summary              : a single sentence summarising the reputation assessment
NA
NA
NA
NA


What changes between prompts
The only difference between the two prompts above is the scope instruction. Prompt A says “base your assessment ONLY on the provided text.” Prompt B says “based on your general knowledge.” This single sentence change produces dramatically different outputs for some banks and identical outputs for others — which is exactly the comparison we are about to run.


Length, multi-turn, and chain of thought
A few practical rules for prompt design:

Be specific, not long. A focused 100-token prompt usually outperforms a vague 500-token one.
Ask for one thing at a time. If you need sentiment and a list of risk factors and a summary, it is fine to ask for all three in one JSON object — but structure them clearly.
Chain of thought: for reasoning tasks, adding “think step by step” before the final answer often improves quality. This is less important at temperature = 0 for simple classification.
Multi-turn: for iterative tasks — “now refine your summary to be more formal” — use the conversation history. Each additional turn costs tokens.
Few-shot examples: providing two or three example input-output pairs in the user prompt dramatically improves consistency for edge cases.

title: TA6: Using AI for research — LLMs in empirical finance
section: What to consider before using LLMs in research
text:
What to consider before using LLMs in research

Cost
LLMs cost money, but less than most people expect. The gpt-4o-mini model costs roughly $0.15 per million input tokens and $0.60 per million output tokens. This TA exercise — 20 API calls analysing bank excerpts — costs about $0.002 in total.
The numbers that matter for a real project:



Task
Cost




100 bank excerpts (~250 tokens each)
~$0.01


100 full 10-K filings (~40,000 tokens each)
~$0.61


1,000 full 10-K filings (~40,000 tokens each)
~$6.09



Two rules: cache every response on disk (so you pay once, not once per pipeline run), and use gpt-4o-mini for classification/extraction tasks — GPT-4o is roughly 15× more expensive per token.


Privacy and data governance
If the data contains material non-public information, pre-publication research findings, or personal data about identifiable individuals, you need explicit clearance from your institution before it leaves your machine. Options when clearance is not available: Azure OpenAI with a private deployment, an open-source model running locally (e.g. llama.cpp, ollama), or restructuring the task so that only non-sensitive metadata leaves your machine.


Reproducibility
Three threats:

Model updates: OpenAI silently updates model weights. Archive the exact model string (e.g. gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18) in your paper’s data appendix, not just gpt-4o-mini.
Temperature > 0: non-zero temperature means non-deterministic outputs. If you use it, run the same prompt multiple times and report the distribution, not a single draw.
Cache loss: if you lose the cached responses, you cannot reproduce the exact outputs. Treat the cache the way you treat your data — version it, back it up.



Hallucinations
LLMs confidently produce false information. For classification tasks on text you provide, hallucinations are rare (the model is constrained by the text). For factual-lookup tasks (specific numbers, dates, citations), hallucinations are common and dangerous. A standard check: if the answer is specific and numerical, verify it against a primary source.

The cost only becomes material when you scale to thousands of long documents (10-K filings, earnings call transcripts).


Step 6: Agents — the next level
A single API call takes one input and returns one output. An agent is a loop: the model generates a tool call, you execute it, feed the result back, and the model generates the next action. This continues until the model signals it is done.
# Conceptual agent loop — pseudocode only, not executed in TA6

# Tools the agent can call:
tools <- list(
  search = function(query) { ... },     # web search or database lookup
  read   = function(url)   { ... },     # fetch and return text
  write  = function(path, text) { ... } # save results to disk
)

# Agent loop:
task <- 'Summarise risk factors for the top 10 US banks from SEC EDGAR'
history <- list(list(role = 'user', content = task))

repeat {
  response <- call_llm(history, tools = tools)
  if (response$type == 'final_answer') break
  # Execute the tool the model requested
  tool_result <- tools[[response$tool]](response$arguments)
  # Feed the result back into the conversation
  history <- append(history, list(list(role = 'tool', content = tool_result)))
}
The practical value of agents for finance research: tasks like “for each of the 500 banks in my sample, find their most recent 10-K on SEC EDGAR, download the risk-factors section, classify the top three risk themes, and write the result to a CSV” can be automated with an agent that chains a search tool, a download tool, and an LLM call. Without agents, you write that pipeline by hand; with agents, you write the task description in English and the model figures out the steps.
R packages that support agent-style loops: ellmer and tidyllm. The OpenAI Responses API supports server-side tool calling directly.


Question for you

You want to classify the sentiment of 10,000 earnings call transcripts. Each transcript is approximately 20,000 tokens. You plan to use gpt-4o-mini at the prices in the cost table above.

Estimate the total input-token cost.
What would you do differently if the transcripts contain material non-public information?
How would you design the caching strategy so the pipeline can be interrupted and resumed without re-paying for calls already made?

title: TA6: Using AI for research — LLMs in empirical finance
text:
What to take away

LLMs are pattern-completion engines, not databases. Use them where a knowledgeable person reading the text would be useful; never use them as a primary source for specific numerical facts.
Temperature = 0 for classification and extraction. Document the exact model version in your data appendix. Cache every response.
Prompt design is the research design. The scope instruction (“based only on this text” vs “based on your general knowledge”) is a methodological choice with real consequences for what you measure.
The comparison table is the result. The interesting signal in TA6 is not “what is Wells Fargo’s sentiment?” — it is “why do the toy dictionary, the excerpt LLM, and the knowledge LLM disagree, and what does that tell us about what each method is actually measuring?”
Agents are the next step: chaining LLM calls with tool-use lets you automate multi-step research pipelines. The infrastructure is available today in R through ellmer and tidyllm.

A few questions to consolidate:

Why does temperature = 0 not guarantee reproducibility across time?
You send a 10-K filing to the OpenAI API. Before doing so, what questions should you ask your institution’s data governance team?
Why did the toy dictionary call TD Bank negative when the excerpt contained no explicit negative language?
What is the difference between an LLM call and an agent? When would you reach for one versus the other?
If a journal referee asks you to reproduce an LLM-based result from three years ago, what should your paper’s data appendix contain?

For further reading:

Lopez-Lira, T., and Y. Tang (2023), “Can ChatGPT Forecast Stock Price Movements? Return Predictability and Large Language Models”, SSRN Working Paper.
Kim, A. G., M. Muhn, and V. V. Nikolaev (2024), “Financial Statement Analysis with Large Language Models”, SSRN Working Paper.
Loughran, T., and B. McDonald (2011), “When is a Liability not a Liability? Textual Analysis, Dictionaries, and 10-Ks”, Journal of Finance 66(1), 35–65.
OpenAI (2024), Prompt Engineering Guide: platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering.

title: TA4: Difference-in-Differences and Staggered DiD
text:
The main empirical question
This TA is built around one concrete question:
What is the effect of crossing the $10bn regulatory threshold on bank loan growth?
The economic story is simple. Once a bank crosses the $10bn asset threshold, it faces stricter regulation, more reporting, and tighter capital and liquidity supervision. We expect the additional regulatory burden to slow lending, at least on average, and especially for cohorts that cross the threshold during years of high regulatory intensity.
Banks cross the threshold in different years. Some banks never cross. That is exactly the textbook setup for staggered DiD.
Concretely, the variables we use are:

bank_id, year: panel identifiers
assets_bn: bank assets, the running variable that triggers the regulatory threshold
loan_growth: outcome of interest
first_treat: first year in which the bank crossed $10bn (0 if never)
treated: 1 once the bank has crossed, 0 otherwise

We drop banks that were already above $10bn in 2012 (no pre-treatment observations) and recode never-treated banks to first_treat_sa = 9999 so that fixest::sunab() recognises them as the never-treated reference group.

title: TA4: Difference-in-Differences and Staggered DiD
text:
DiD: the basic logic
Start with the cleanest case. Two groups (treated and control), two periods (pre and post). The DiD estimator is simply the difference between two changes:
\[
\widehat{\beta}_{DiD} = \big( \bar{Y}_{T,\text{post}} - \bar{Y}_{T,\text{pre}} \big) - \big( \bar{Y}_{C,\text{post}} - \bar{Y}_{C,\text{pre}} \big).
\]
In words: take the change in the outcome for the treated group, subtract the change in the outcome for the control group, and report what is left. The same idea, written as a regression, is the textbook fixed-effects DiD:
\[
Y_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \gamma_t + \beta \cdot D_{i,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t},
\]
with unit fixed effects \(\alpha_i\), time fixed effects \(\gamma_t\), and a treatment dummy \(D_{i,t}\) that switches on after treatment. The coefficient \(\beta\) is the DiD estimate.

The identifying assumption: parallel trends
DiD does not assume that treated and control units are identical. It assumes that, in the absence of treatment, their outcomes would have moved in parallel. Formally, with \(Y(0)\) denoting the potential outcome under no treatment:
\[
\mathbb{E}\big[Y(0)_{T,\text{post}} - Y(0)_{T,\text{pre}}\big] = \mathbb{E}\big[Y(0)_{C,\text{post}} - Y(0)_{C,\text{pre}}\big].
\]
This statement is untestable, since the left-hand side is a counterfactual we never observe. The only evidence we can collect for it is indirect: pre-treatment trends should look parallel, the economic story should make divergent counterfactuals implausible, and placebo or anticipation tests should not flag anything obvious. A common confusion is to read parallel trends as “treated and controls have the same level”. It is not about the level. It is about the slope of the counterfactual path.


What DiD identifies: the ATT
If parallel trends holds, DiD identifies the average treatment effect on the treated (ATT):
\[
ATT = \mathbb{E}\big[Y(1) - Y(0) \,\big|\, \text{treated}\big].
\]
This is a smaller and more honest object than the ATE. DiD speaks only about the treated group: it asks how much their outcome changed, compared with the trend the control group suggests would have happened to them in the absence of treatment.

title: TA4: Difference-in-Differences and Staggered DiD
text:
Staggered DiD: when timing varies
In our application, banks cross the $10bn threshold in different years. Some never cross. So instead of one “treated group” and one “control group”, we have a control group of never-treated banks plus many treatment cohorts, indexed by the year they first crossed the threshold.
The natural extension of the regression DiD is the standard two-way fixed-effects (TWFE) specification, with \(D_{i,t}\) now switching on the year a bank first crosses the threshold:
\[
Y_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \gamma_t + \beta \cdot D_{i,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t}.
\]
It is tempting to read \(\beta\) as “the average effect of crossing the threshold”. The literature has shown that with staggered timing this reading is wrong in general.

Why TWFE can be biased
The Goodman-Bacon (2021) decomposition shows that the TWFE coefficient is a weighted average of every possible 2x2 DiD that you can build from the panel. There are three kinds of 2x2 building blocks:

Treated vs never-treated. Clean comparison, gives the ATT we want.
Earlier-treated vs later-treated (during the period when the later cohort is still untreated). The later-treated act as controls before they are treated. Still clean.
Later-treated vs earlier-treated (during the period when the earlier cohort is already treated). The earlier-treated act as controls for the later-treated, even though they are themselves already treated. This is the “forbidden comparison”.

The third building block is the problem. If the earlier-treated cohort is still adjusting to its own treatment (dynamic effects) or if its treatment effect is different from that of later cohorts (heterogeneity), then using them as controls contaminates the comparison. TWFE can also put negative weights on some 2x2 building blocks. The headline coefficient is then not interpretable as the ATT, and in extreme cases it can have the wrong sign.
A practical way to detect the problem is to estimate TWFE and a heterogeneity-robust estimator (Sun & Abraham, Callaway & Sant’Anna, did2s, …), then compare them. If the two disagree noticeably, TWFE is contaminated. If they agree, the bias is empirically small in your sample.


What Sun & Abraham (2021) does differently
Sun & Abraham define a separate ATT for each combination of cohort \(g\) (the year a bank is first treated) and event time \(\ell\) (years since first treatment):
\[
ATT(g, \ell) = \mathbb{E}\big[Y_{i, g+\ell}(1) - Y_{i, g+\ell}(0) \,\big|\, G_i = g\big].
\]
They estimate each \(ATT(g, \ell)\) using only the corresponding cohort and never-treated (or not-yet-treated) units as controls. Already-treated units are never used as controls. The cohort-specific effects are then aggregated with non-negative weights, which gives a clean event-study profile \(\widehat{ATT}(\ell)\) and a clean aggregate ATT.
The fixest function sunab(cohort, period) implements this directly inside feols(). The never-treated reference cohort must take a value larger than the maximum period of the panel, which is why we recode never-treated banks to 9999. If you forget this step, sunab() will silently treat the wrong cohort as the reference and the event-study plot will look strange.

title: TA4: Difference-in-Differences and Staggered DiD
section: Where DiD fits, and common mistakes
text:
Where DiD fits, and common mistakes
DiD is one of several identification strategies you have seen in this course. It is the right tool when treatment timing varies across units and you have a credible never-treated (or later-treated) control group.








Strategy
Source of variation
Best when




OLS / fixed effects
Within-unit variation
Treatment is plausibly exogenous conditional on observables and FEs


Event study (TA2)
Event-window variation
A discrete event with a clean window


Instrumental variables (TA3)
Variation from an external instrument
The regressor is endogenous and you have a valid instrument


Difference-in-differences
Treatment-control × pre-post variation
A policy / event affects some units (or affects them earlier)


Regression discontinuity
Variation around a threshold
Treatment is a deterministic function of a running variable near a cutoff



Even when DiD is the right tool, it is easy to misuse. The most common mistakes are:

Treating TWFE on staggered data as a clean ATT. With staggered timing and heterogeneous effects this is biased. Always check with a heterogeneity-robust estimator.
Reading parallel-trends as “treated and controls have the same level”. Parallel trends is about the slopes of the counterfactual, not the levels.
Including always-treated units in an event study. Without pre-treatment observations they cannot identify a clean event-study coefficient. Drop them.
Forgetting to cluster. Standard errors should be clustered at the level of treatment assignment (here, the bank).
Looking only at significance. A statistically significant TWFE coefficient that disappears under Sun & Abraham is a warning sign, not a confirmation.
Selecting the post-treatment window after seeing the data. Decide the event window in advance.

Time to code
source("code/00_utils.R")
load_finance4_packages()

  dplyr::filter(first_treat == 0L | first_treat >= 2013L) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    first_treat_sa = dplyr::if_else(
      first_treat == 0L, 9999L, as.integer(first_treat)
    )
  )
The full script is code/05_ta4_staggered_did.R. The remainder of this section walks through every result the script produces, in the same order it produces them.

Step 1: Treatment cohorts in the panel
The first thing we do after loading the panel is count how many banks fall into each cohort. This is the table the script writes to output/tables/ta4_cohort_summary.csv.

The “Never treated” group is our cleanest control group. Each “Treated in YYYY” row is its own treatment cohort with its own pre and post periods. The fact that no single year contains the entire treated population is what makes this a staggered design and why a single TWFE coefficient is suspicious by default.


Step 2: Treatment timing across banks
Next, we visualise the staggered structure directly. Each row is a bank, each column is a year, and the colour indicates whether the bank is treated in that year.

Cohorts switch on at different years and never-treated banks remain grey throughout. This is also the picture the Goodman-Bacon decomposition is operating on: every horizontal pair of rows produces a 2x2 building block, and TWFE averages all of them with weights that depend on cohort sizes and on how long each cohort spends treated.


Step 3: Pre-treatment trends across cohorts
Before any regression, we look at the raw mean of loan_growth by cohort and year. This is a descriptive plausibility check for parallel trends, not a formal test.

We are looking at two things here:

whether the lines are roughly parallel before each cohort’s treatment year, and
whether the lines diverge after treatment.

If the pre-treatment lines drift systematically apart, the parallel-trends story becomes harder to defend and any DiD interpretation is on shaky ground. In our sample the pre-treatment lines move together reasonably well, which gives us licence to take the post-treatment divergence at face value.


Step 4: Naive TWFE estimate
Now the workhorse regression. The TWFE specification we estimate is:
\[
loan\_growth_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \gamma_t + \beta \cdot treated_{i,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t}.
\]
This is the “default” DiD spec everyone writes down, and it produces a single number for \(\beta\). Because of the staggered structure, we should treat that number as a diagnostic and not as the ATT.

The honest reading is: “TWFE points in the direction the regulatory story predicts, but it is not by itself decisive, and we have not yet addressed the staggered-timing bias.” The next step does both.


Step 5: Sun & Abraham event-study coefficients
We re-estimate the same panel using fixest::sunab(first_treat_sa, year). This produces one coefficient per event time, weighted across cohorts. The negative event times are pre-treatment placebos; the non-negative event times are the dynamic ATTs.

The coefficients before event time 0 are mostly small and statistically indistinguishable from zero. That is reassuring evidence in favour of parallel trends.

Step 6: Event-study plot
The same coefficients become much easier to read once we plot them with their 95% confidence intervals.

How to read it:

Event time = 0 is the year the bank first crosses $10bn.
Coefficients to the left of the dotted line are pre-treatment placebos. They should hover around zero if parallel trends is plausible.
Coefficients to the right of the dotted line are the dynamic ATTs. A flat negative profile would suggest a constant negative effect; a downward-sloping profile would suggest an effect that grows over time.

In our sample, the pre-treatment placebos are flat around zero and the post-treatment coefficients are slightly negative but noisy. That is exactly the picture we would expect from a regulatory friction that quietly slows lending without producing a dramatic break.


Step 7: TWFE versus Sun & Abraham
This is the headline diagnostic. We put the naive TWFE coefficient and the Sun & Abraham aggregate ATT side by side, with their 95% confidence intervals.

The number we report as our headline causal estimate is the Sun & Abraham aggregate ATT, with TWFE kept as a cross-check.

Suppose you reran this exact analysis on a panel where treatment effects grew much faster with event time, and where most banks were treated early in the sample. What would you expect to happen to the gap between the TWFE coefficient and the Sun & Abraham aggregate ATT?

title: TA4: Difference-in-Differences and Staggered DiD
text:
What to take away
A few things to internalise from this TA:

DiD identifies the ATT, under the parallel-trends assumption. It does not identify the ATE, and it does not require treated and control units to be identical in levels.
With staggered timing, the standard TWFE coefficient mixes good and bad 2x2 comparisons. When the bias is empirically large, the headline number changes when you switch to a heterogeneity-robust estimator.
Always report a TWFE estimate and at least one staggered-DiD estimator (Sun & Abraham, Callaway & Sant’Anna, …), plus an event-study plot. The TWFE coefficient is a diagnostic, not the final answer.
Cluster standard errors at the level of treatment assignment, drop always-treated units, decide the event window before looking at results, and remember that pre-treatment placebos are evidence about parallel trends, not a formal test.

A few questions you should be able to answer at this stage:

Why is parallel trends untestable, and what do we use as evidence in its favour?
What exactly is the “forbidden comparison” in Goodman-Bacon’s decomposition?
Why must never-treated cohorts be coded above the maximum period for fixest::sunab() to recognise them?
If the event-study plot showed a clear downward jump at event time = 0 followed by a flat profile, what would you conclude about the dynamic structure of the effect?
If the pre-treatment placebos in the event-study plot were systematically negative, what would that imply about the design?

For the formal references behind everything above, the staggered-DiD literature is now relatively self-contained:

Goodman-Bacon (2021), “Difference-in-differences with variation in treatment timing”, Journal of Econometrics.
Sun & Abraham (2021), “Estimating dynamic treatment effects in event studies with heterogeneous treatment effects”, Journal of Econometrics.
Callaway & Sant’Anna (2021), “Difference-in-differences with multiple time periods”, Journal of Econometrics.
Baker, Larcker & Wang (2022), “How much should we trust staggered difference-in-differences estimates?”, Journal of Financial Economics.
Roth, Sant’Anna, Bilinski & Poe (2023), “What’s trending in difference-in-differences? A synthesis of the recent econometrics literature”, Journal of Econometrics.

title: TA2: Event Studies Through Regulatory Announcements
section: Why event studies are central in finance
text:
Why event studies are central in finance
Event studies are one of the clearest ways to connect new information to firm value.
The basic idea is simple: when markets learn something important, prices should adjust quickly. That is why event studies are especially useful in corporate finance, banking, and asset pricing. Accounting variables often move slowly, but valuation effects can appear immediately in market prices.
This is the conceptual shift from TA1 to TA2:

TA1: slow-moving outcomes such as lending, balance sheets, and profitability
TA2: fast-moving market expectations embedded in stock prices

If a supervisory announcement reveals that some banks will face tighter constraints, higher compliance costs, or worse future cash flows, investors should incorporate that information right away.

title: TA2: Event Studies Through Regulatory Announcements
section: What an event study is trying to identify
text:
What an event study is trying to identify
An event study is trying to isolate the unexpected valuation effect of a specific information release.
In notation, the object of interest is the abnormal return:
\[
AR_{i,t} = R_{i,t} - \mathbb{E}[R_{i,t} \mid \text{market conditions}]
\]
This is the part of the return that is not explained by normal market-wide movements.
A cumulative abnormal return over an event window \((\tau_1, \tau_2)\) is:
\[
CAR_{i}(\tau_1,\tau_2) = \sum_{\tau=\tau_1}^{\tau_2} AR_{i,\tau}
\]
So the event-study question is not just “did returns move?” It is:
Did returns move in an unusual way when the market learned the news?

title: TA2: Event Studies Through Regulatory Announcements
text:
The core identification logic
For an event study to be persuasive, three things need to be approximately true.

1. The event date must be clean
The announcement must arrive at a well-defined point in time. If the timing is vague, identification becomes weak.


2. The window must be short
Short windows help us attribute the price movement to the event rather than to unrelated news.


3. The market must not have fully priced the information earlier
If the announcement was anticipated, leaked, or gradually revealed, the measured day-0 reaction will understate the true economic effect.






Remember!



In a full empirical project, we would estimate expected returns using market data (WRDS, CRSP) and then construct abnormal returns from raw returns. In this synthetic exercise, abnormal returns are already embedded in the data-generating process, so we can focus on research design, interpretation, and implementation.








Bottom line



A credible event study needs a clearly timed piece of news, a short return window, and limited anticipation. If those conditions hold, differences in abnormal returns around the announcement can be interpreted as differences in how the market values the news.

title: TA2: Event Studies Through Regulatory Announcements
text:
What can threaten identification?
Three issues are especially important in event studies:

Anticipation: investors may have expected the announcement before day 0.
Leakage: some information may have reached the market before the formal release.
Confounding news: other relevant events may occur in the same window.

These concerns are exactly why event studies rely on sharp timing and short windows.

title: TA2: Event Studies Through Regulatory Announcements
text:
Key concepts

1. Event date and event window
The event date is the supervisory announcement. We then study returns in a narrow window around that date.
Common windows in this TA are:

Day 0 AR: the abnormal return on the announcement day
CAR(-1,+1): cumulative abnormal return from one day before to one day after
CAR(0,+2): cumulative abnormal return from the event day through two days after
CAR(-2,+2): a slightly wider short-run window

Why not use a long window? Because long windows make attribution harder.


2. Abnormal return versus CAR

Abnormal return captures the surprise component on a single day.
CAR aggregates abnormal returns over a short interval.

Single-day returns can be noisy. CARs are often easier to interpret because they allow for slight timing frictions, delayed reactions, or information leakage just before the announcement.


3. Exposure-based comparison
A natural first step is to compare firms that should be more affected by the event with firms that should be less affected.
In this TA, we split banks into:

High fragility
Low fragility

This gives a transparent first test of heterogeneous exposure: do more fragile banks react more negatively around the announcement?


4. Cross-sectional event-study regression
The regression turns the visual and tabular comparison into a more disciplined empirical test.
It asks whether banks with higher fragility experience worse event reactions after controlling for:

capital ratios
political connections
treatment status

Year fixed effects absorb the average intensity of the announcement in each event year.

title: TA2: Event Studies Through Regulatory Announcements

code/02_ta2_event_study.R

plot average abnormal returns and cumulative abnormal returns by fragility group
collapse the data to one row per bank-event
test whether high-fragility and low-fragility banks react differently

title: TA1: Workflow in empirical research
section: How to think about the empirical aspect of research
text:
How to think about the empirical aspect of research
Empirical research is not “running regressions.” It is building a credible bridge from variation in the world to a precise claim, and making that bridge strong enough to survive a skeptical referee.

1) Start with an estimand, not a specification
Before code, write down the object you want to study:

Causal estimand: the effect of (X) on (Y) (under a clearly stated intervention).
Descriptive estimand: how (X) and (Y) co-move (useful, but not causal).
Predictive estimand: forecasting (Y) (rarely the main goal in this course).

A regression is one possible estimator. The estimand is the research.


2) Identification is a story about variation
The core question is always:

Which variation identifies your claim?

In practice:

What moves your “treatment” variable?
Why is that movement plausibly unrelated to the outcome except through your channel?
What would break this argument?

If you cannot answer these three, you do not have an empirical design yet.


3) Assumptions are the price you pay for inference
Every design buys inference by paying assumptions. Your job is to:

minimize the assumptions,
state them clearly,
test their implications wherever possible.

For example, fixed effects do not “solve endogeneity.” They change the comparison!


4) Data construction is part of the design
In corporate finance, many results are driven by measurement and sample choices:

definitions (capital, liquidity, lending),
winsorization and missing data rules,
the sample pipeline (who enters, who exits, mergers, breakpoints).

A credible workflow produces:

a transparent construction pipeline,
a variable dictionary,
a one-command replication script.



5) Estimation and inference are engineering
Once the design is clear, implementation must be correct:

fixed effects reflect the comparison you want,
clustering reflects the correlation structure of shocks,
robustness checks target specific threats, not a grab bag of controls.

Code is not decoration: it is the proof that you estimated what you claimed.


6) Interpretation is economic, not statistical
“Significant” is not the endpoint.
Translate coefficients into:

units that matter (percent, bps, dollars),
plausible mechanisms,
magnitudes relative to benchmarks.

If you cannot explain a coefficient in one sentence, the design is not finished.


Referee-mode checklist (use this every time)
You are ready to show results only if you can answer:

What is the claim / estimand?

What variation identifies it?

Which assumption does the heavy lifting?

What diagnostic would convince you that you are wrong?

title: TA1: Workflow in empirical research
text:
How to organize empirical workflow
A good empirical workflow is not “clean data → run regressions.” It is a repeatable pipeline that turns raw inputs into claims, with enough structure that (i) you can debug, (ii) you can extend, and (iii) someone else can replicate.

The principle: one button replication
Your project should have a single entry point:

run_all.R rebuilds everything from scratch (data → tables/figures → site).
If run_all.R breaks, the project is not yet research-grade.



Recommended project structure
Use a consistent folder layout:

data_raw/ — original inputs (never edited by hand)
data_clean/ — cleaned intermediate data (created by scripts)
code/ — scripts (numbered in execution order)
output/tables/ — exported tables (CSV/LaTeX)
output/figures/ — exported figures (PNG/PDF)
docs/ — rendered site (GitHub Pages target)
scratch/ — temporary experiments (safe to delete)

Rule: only data_raw/ is “source of truth.” Everything else is reproducible output.


The pipeline (design → data → estimation → reporting)
Organize scripts as a sequence:

00_setup.R
load packages, set paths, set seed, define global options
01_build_data.R
read raw inputs, clean, construct variables, write data_clean/master_panel.csv
02_descriptives.R
sample checks, missingness, summary tables, baseline plots
03_estimation.R
main specifications, inference choices (FE, clustering), robustness targeted to threats
04_outputs.R
export model tables, export figures, save objects needed by the site
05_render.R
render the website / report

This is exactly how you keep analysis modular and debuggable.


“Logs” you should always produce
Every serious empirical project prints (and saves) the answers to:

What is the sample size at each filter step?
How many unique units (banks / firms) and time periods?
Are there duplicates in the panel key?
What is missing, and is it systematic?
What is the distribution of key variables (levels and changes)?

If you do not log these, you will waste days later.


Version control is part of the workflow
A clean Git routine:

commit after each meaningful, working change
commit messages describe what changed and why
keep large derived files out of Git (use .gitignore for data_clean/ if needed)
tag stable versions (e.g., v1_ta1_complete) so you can always revert



Dependency management (avoid “it works on my machine”)
Do not install packages inside scripts.

use renv (or equivalent) to lock package versions
record session info at the end of runs (sessionInfo())



The TA1 standard
In TA1, the workflow goal is:

Build a synthetic master panel once, then repeatedly reuse it for descriptives and fixed-effects models.

By TA2–TA6, you should still be using the same pipeline — you just add modules and extend the master dataset.

title: TA1: Workflow in empirical research
section: What is the most appropriate coding language for my project?
text:
What is the most appropriate coding language for my project?
The “best” language is the one that minimizes total research cost: time to implement + time to debug + time to replicate + credibility with your audience.

Quick positioning

Stata: best for standard applied micro workflows and fast, conventional regression work; weakest for modern software engineering and large-scale data pipelines.
R: best all-around for econometrics + visualization + reproducible reporting; strong for high-dimensional fixed effects and modern causal packages.
Python: best for data engineering, web scraping, APIs, text/NLP, and scalable pipelines; econometrics is good but reporting and some “applied micro conventions” can be more work than in Stata/R.
Gretl: great teaching tool and lightweight econometrics; less common in top-tier research workflows and ecosystem is smaller.
Matlab: excellent for numerical methods, simulations, structural models, optimization; not the default for reduced-form empirical pipelines (data cleaning + causal inference + reporting).



What each one is “for” in practice

Stata

you want the quickest path from cleaned panel → conventional tables,
you are following a classic applied micro/corp-fin template,
your coauthors/advisors are Stata-native.

Watch-outs: less natural for reproducible research engineering (project pipelines, dependency mgmt), more friction for scraping, APIs, and complex text processing.


R

you want research-grade reporting (Quarto/Rmd) + clean figures + tables,
you need high-dimensional fixed effects and modern causal inference tooling,
you want to teach / enforce a transparent workflow (scripts + outputs + site).

Watch-outs:

some data engineering tasks are easier in Python (scraping, messy parsing),
performance can require care for very large datasets.



Python

you need web scraping, APIs, NLP, and messy data ingestion,
you want a scalable pipeline mindset (testing, automation),
you’re building datasets more than running canned regressions.

Watch-outs:

for “applied micro style” regression tables and FE-heavy work, you may do more manual setup than in R/Stata,
the ecosystem is broader, so conventions vary more across labs.



Gretl

you want a simple environment for classic econometrics and teaching,
you need something lightweight and transparent for small projects.

Watch-outs:

much smaller ecosystem; less common in corp-fin research replication packages,
fewer cutting-edge methods implemented as quickly as R/Stata.



Matlab

you are doing structural estimation, numerical optimization, simulations, dynamic programming,
you need high-quality linear algebra tools and controlled numerical workflows.

Watch-outs:

not the easiest default for data cleaning + causal inference + reproducible reporting,
fewer “applied micro defaults” than Stata/R.




A good rule for this course
For Finance 4 empirical TA work, the lowest-friction, highest-credibility stack is:

R as the main analysis language (econometrics + reporting + figures)
Git for version control
Python as an add-on when we move to web scraping / text (later topics)

If you personally live in Stata, a perfectly fine alternative is:

Stata for estimation + core tables, plus
Python (or R) for scraping and data construction, and
still Git for version control.



The only “wrong” choice
Mixing languages without clear boundaries.
Good mixes:

Python → R (Python builds data, R estimates/reports)
Python → Stata (Python builds data, Stata estimates/tables)
SQL → (R/Stata/Python) (SQL builds analysis tables)

Bad mix:

switching languages mid-analysis for the same task (harder to replicate, harder to debug, harder to teach).

Time to Code!

Data
TA1 uses a synthetic banking panel so everyone can run the full workflow locally without proprietary access, with:

one observation per bank-year,

Core Idea
Fixed effects are not a magic solution to endogeneity. They answer a different question:

pooled OLS compares different banks to one another,
bank fixed effects compare a bank to itself over time,
year fixed effects purge common aggregate shocks.



Intuition
When we study lending, we usually do not observe pure credit supply. Loan growth reflects:

bank willingness to lend,
borrower demand for credit,
common macro conditions,
local shocks in the places where the bank operates.

title: TA3: IV and the Causal Effect of Local Credit Supply
text:
The main empirical question
This TA is built around one simple question:
What is the causal effect of local bank credit supply on county employment growth?
This maps naturally to the real economy:

if banks pull back credit supply, local firms may hire less
if banks expand credit supply, local employment may grow faster

The problem is that observed local credit growth is not pure supply.

Why OLS is biased
Suppose a county is booming.

firms want to borrow more
banks lend more
employment rises

Then observed credit growth and employment growth move together even if the true causal effect of credit supply is smaller than the raw correlation suggests.
That is the core endogeneity problem:

Local demand affects both the regressor and the outcome.

So OLS does not recover a clean supply effect.
More formally, suppose the object of interest is:
\[
employment\_growth_{c,t} = \beta \cdot credit\_supply_{c,t} + u_{c,t}
\]
but the variable we actually observe is:
\[
observed\_credit\_growth_{c,t} = credit\_supply_{c,t} + demand_{c,t}
\]
If demand also affects employment growth, then regressing employment growth on observed credit growth mixes:

the effect of credit supply
the effect of local demand

That is why the OLS coefficient cannot be interpreted mechanically as causal.

title: TA3: IV and the Causal Effect of Local Credit Supply
text:
Endogeneity: the core problem
An endogenous regressor is a regressor that is correlated with the error term in the outcome equation.
In this application, observed_credit_growth is endogenous because it reflects both:

changes in local bank credit supply
changes in local credit demand

If local demand is omitted from the regression but still affects employment growth, then the regressor is correlated with the omitted component of the outcome equation. That is the precise sense in which OLS is biased.
This is why the issue is not that OLS is “bad” in general. The issue is that OLS needs variation in the regressor that is not contaminated by omitted determinants of the outcome.

title: TA3: IV and the Causal Effect of Local Credit Supply
section: The IV idea in this TA
text:
The IV idea in this TA
We use an instrument based on counties’ exposure to banks that were more fragile when a common rate shock hit.
The economic story is:

some counties are more exposed to fragile banks
the common rate shock hits fragile banks harder
those counties experience a worse local credit-supply shock

So the instrument is designed to generate plausibly exogenous variation in local credit growth.

What an instrumental variable is
An instrumental variable is a variable Z that helps us isolate the part of an endogenous regressor X that is plausibly unrelated to the omitted confounders in the outcome equation.
In this TA:

X = observed_credit_growth
Y = employment_growth
Z = instrument_z



When do we use IV?
IV is useful when the regressor of interest is endogenous and we do not trust the identifying variation used by OLS.
Typical reasons a regressor may be endogenous include:

omitted variables: some unobserved factor affects both X and Y
reverse causality: Y may also affect X
measurement error: the regressor is measured with error

IV is one way to replace contaminated variation in X with a cleaner source of variation coming from Z.
So the question is not:

“Should we use IV because it is more advanced?”

The right question is:

“Do we have a believable source of exogenous variation that can isolate the part of X we care about?”



What makes a good instrument?
A good instrument is not just something correlated with X. It must also be economically interpretable.
In practice, a persuasive instrument usually has:

a clear economic story for why it moves X
a clear argument for why it does not directly move Y
timing or institutional structure that makes the variation believable
enough variation to generate a non-trivial first stage

This is why good IV designs are hard to find. The challenge is usually not running 2SLS. The challenge is defending the instrument.
An ideal instrument must satisfy two conditions:

1. Relevance
The instrument must move the endogenous regressor.
Here that means:

Counties with greater exposure to fragile banks should experience different local credit growth when the common rate shock hits.



2. Exclusion
The instrument should affect the outcome only through the endogenous regressor.
Here that means:

Exposure to fragile banks should affect county employment growth only because it changes local credit supply, not because it directly changes county employment through some other channel.




What IV is not
It is important to be clear about what IV does not do.
IV is not:

a generic fix for every bad regression
a way to avoid thinking carefully about economics
a method that automatically guarantees causality once the first stage is significant

IV only helps if the instrument is both relevant and excludable. If the economic story behind the instrument is weak, 2SLS does not rescue the design.


Why fixed effects are not enough here
County fixed effects absorb time-invariant county characteristics such as persistent industrial composition or geography. Year fixed effects absorb common aggregate shocks.
But neither of those fully solves the central problem if counties still experience time-varying local demand shocks. If a county has an especially strong local boom in a given year, that can still increase both lending and employment at the same time.
So fixed effects help, but they do not by themselves produce a clean credit-supply estimate.






Interpretation



The instrument is not “credit growth itself.” It is a source of variation that shifts credit growth for reasons tied to bank fragility rather than county demand.

title: TA3: IV and the Causal Effect of Local Credit Supply
text:
IV versus other identification strategies
It is useful to compare IV to the methods we have already seen or will see later:

Fixed effects remove time-invariant omitted variables, but not all time-varying confounders.
Event studies use sharply timed information shocks and price reactions.
Difference-in-differences compares treated and untreated units over time under a parallel-trends assumption.
RDD uses local quasi-random variation around a threshold.
IV uses an outside source of variation that shifts the endogenous regressor.

So IV is not “better” than every other design. It answers a different identification problem. We use it when the key challenge is that the regressor is endogenous, but we have a plausible external shifter.

What IV is doing conceptually
The IV design breaks into three parts:

First stage: does the instrument move local credit growth?
Reduced form: does the instrument move employment growth?
2SLS: what is the effect of the instrument-induced part of credit growth on employment growth?

This is the order we follow in the code and in the results below.
It is useful to think of IV as a two-step logic:

first, isolate the part of credit growth that comes from the instrument
second, ask what that isolated component does to employment growth

So 2SLS does not use all variation in credit growth. It uses only the variation in credit growth that is explained by the instrument.
Another way to say this is:

OLS asks: what happens to Y when X changes for any reason?
IV asks: what happens to Y when X changes for the specific reason captured by Z?

That is why IV estimates can differ from OLS estimates even when both are statistically precise.


The three key equations
It is useful to write the design in equation form.

OLS equation
\[
employment\_growth_{c,t} = \beta \cdot observed\_credit\_growth_{c,t} + \alpha_c + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{c,t}
\]
This is the naive specification. It is biased if observed_credit_growth is correlated with \varepsilon.


First stage
\[
observed\_credit\_growth_{c,t} = \pi \cdot instrument\_z_{c,t} + \alpha_c + \gamma_t + \nu_{c,t}
\]
This checks whether the instrument moves the endogenous regressor.


Reduced form
\[
employment\_growth_{c,t} = \rho \cdot instrument\_z_{c,t} + \alpha_c + \gamma_t + \eta_{c,t}
\]
This checks whether the instrument also moves the outcome.


2SLS
2SLS combines the first stage and reduced form to estimate the effect of the part of credit growth that is predicted by the instrument.
In many simple settings, it is helpful to think of 2SLS as:
\[
\text{2SLS estimate} \approx \frac{\text{reduced form}}{\text{first stage}}
\]
This is not just a computational trick. It shows the intuition:

how much does the instrument move the outcome?
how much does the instrument move the endogenous regressor?
the ratio tells us how much the outcome changes per unit of instrument-induced movement in the regressor

This ratio intuition helps explain why the first stage matters so much.

title: TA3: IV and the Causal Effect of Local Credit Supply
section: What is testable and what is not
text:
What is testable and what is not
Some parts of an IV design can be checked directly in the data, and some cannot.

Things we can partially check

whether the first stage is strong enough to be meaningful
whether the sign of the reduced form is economically sensible
whether results are stable across specifications



Things we cannot directly prove from the data

the exclusion restriction

The exclusion restriction is ultimately an economic argument, not a purely statistical result. That is why the interpretation of the instrument matters so much.

title: TA3: IV and the Causal Effect of Local Credit Supply
text:
Common mistakes in IV reasoning
Students often make a few predictable mistakes when first learning IV:

Mistake 1: “A significant first stage proves the instrument is valid.”
No. A significant first stage only helps with relevance. It says nothing by itself about exclusion.


Mistake 2: “IV gives the true effect of X on Y.”
Not necessarily. IV gives the effect for the variation in X induced by the instrument, not for every possible source of variation in X.


Mistake 3: “If OLS and IV differ, IV must be right.”
Not automatically. The difference may reflect endogeneity, but it could also reflect a weak or invalid instrument. The economic argument still matters.


Mistake 4: “If fixed effects are included, IV is unnecessary.”
No. Fixed effects and IV solve different problems. Fixed effects absorb some omitted variables; IV replaces the variation in the endogenous regressor with a cleaner source.


Weak instruments
A weak instrument is an instrument that only weakly moves the endogenous regressor.
Why this matters:

the first stage becomes noisy
2SLS estimates become unstable
inference becomes less reliable

So a strong first stage is necessary. However, it is not sufficient: an instrument can be strong and still fail the exclusion restriction.

title: TA3: IV and the Causal Effect of Local Credit Supply
text:
What 2SLS identifies
The 2SLS estimate is not “the effect of all lending on all counties in all circumstances.”
It is the effect of credit growth for the part of lending that is shifted by the instrument. In IV language, this is a local average treatment effect.
In this TA, the interpretation is:

the effect of local credit supply on employment growth for counties whose lending conditions move because they are more exposed to fragile banks when the common rate shock hits.

This is a more precise and more honest interpretation than saying simply “the effect of lending on employment.”

title: TA3: IV and the Causal Effect of Local Credit Supply
text:
Time to Code!
We use:

code/04_ta3_iv_credit_supply.R

The script proceeds in the same order as the economic logic:

describe the county panel
show why OLS is not enough
estimate the first stage
estimate the reduced form
estimate the 2SLS model

iv_model <- fixest::feols(
  employment_growth ~ 1 | county_fips + year | observed_credit_growth ~ instrument_z,
  cluster = ~county_fips,
  data = county_panel
)

Step 1: design summary
This table gives a quick feel for how counties differ depending on whether they have high or low exposure to the instrument.

  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 3)))

knitr::kable(design_summary, caption = "Counties with high and low instrument exposure")

The point of this table is not to prove the design by itself. It is simply to make the instrument concrete before looking at regressions.


Step 2: naive OLS relationship
Before using IV, look at the raw correlation. This is what OLS is trying to summarize.

The danger is that this relationship mixes:

credit supply
local demand

So it is not automatically causal.
This is the motivation for IV. If local demand pushes both lending and employment upward, the OLS slope will generally be biased.


Step 3: first stage
Now we ask whether the instrument moves local credit growth in the expected direction.

If the first stage is weak, the whole IV design becomes fragile.
In other words, IV only makes sense if the instrument actually shifts the endogenous regressor in a meaningful way.


Step 4: reduced form
The reduced form asks whether the instrument moves the outcome.
This step is useful because it connects the instrument directly to the economic object of interest:

if the instrument shifts local credit growth
and the instrument also shifts employment growth

then 2SLS combines those two relationships into an estimate of the causal effect of local credit supply on employment.


Step 5: ordered main results
The table below is deliberately ordered exactly as the code creates the results:

title: TA3: IV and the Causal Effect of Local Credit Supply
section: What to take away from this TA
text:
What to take away from this TA
The main lesson is not “IV is more advanced than OLS.” The main lesson is:

When the regressor is endogenous, we need a source of variation that moves the regressor for reasons unrelated to the omitted confounders.

In this application:

OLS is biased because local demand affects both lending and employment
the instrument shifts local credit growth through bank fragility and the common rate shock
2SLS uses that instrument-driven variation to estimate a causal effect

title: TA5: Introduction to web scraping
text:
The main empirical question
This TA is built around a single concrete question:
What can we learn about the largest US banks by scraping public web data, and how do we do it safely, ethically, and reproducibly?
The web is the largest and least-curated dataset humanity has ever assembled. A surprising fraction of recent finance papers — Adams & Mosk (2023) on letters to Congress, Akcigit, Baslandze & Lotti (2023) on political connections in patents, Akey, Heimer & Lewellen (2021) on politicised consumer credit, Babenko, Fedaseyeu & Zhang (2020) on CEO political donations — rely on data that exists only because someone scraped it. None of those papers had a paid Bloomberg-like data feed; they all sat down with HTML pages and parsed them.
For our example we scrape Wikipedia’s List of largest banks in the United States, follow the links to each bank’s individual article, and assemble a small text corpus we can do real analysis on. Wikipedia is an honest teaching example because:

it is explicitly OK to scrape under the CC-BY-SA licence (with attribution),
it is stable — the wikitable structure and the URL scheme barely change from year to year,
it lets us combine structured data (the table) with unstructured data (the article text), which is exactly the workflow most finance scraping projects end up using.

title: TA5: Introduction to web scraping
text:
Web scraping
A web page is just a tree of HTML tags. Your browser receives that tree, runs any JavaScript attached to it, and renders the result. A scraper does the first step (download the tree) and skips the second (rendering) — which is why static scraping works only when the data you want is already in the response.

The rvest workflow
In R the canonical static-scraping pipeline is three function calls:
page <- xml2::read_html("https://example.com/some-page")

table <- page |>
  rvest::html_elements("table.wikitable") |>
  rvest::html_table(fill = TRUE) |>
  purrr::pluck(1)

paragraphs <- page |>
  rvest::html_elements("div.mw-parser-output > p") |>
  rvest::html_text2()
Three things to internalise:

CSS selectors are the language we use to point at HTML elements. table.wikitable means “every <table> whose class includes wikitable”. div.mw-parser-output > p means “every <p> that is an immediate child of <div class='mw-parser-output'>”. The browser developer tools give you these selectors for free — right-click any element and choose Inspect.
html_elements() returns a set of nodes, html_element() returns a single node. They have the same name minus an s and the difference matters once you start nesting walks.
html_text2() is almost always what you want for human-readable text. html_text() keeps every \n and \t exactly as they appear in the source, which produces ugly output 90% of the time.



When rvest is enough — and when it is not
rvest is enough whenever the data you want is in the HTML response. You can check this in 30 seconds: open the page in the browser, view the page source (Cmd-U / Ctrl-U), and search the source for the value you can see on the page. If it is there, rvest will find it. If the search returns nothing, the page is rendered dynamically by JavaScript and you need a real browser, which is what chromote and RSelenium are for. We come back to this in the When you need a real browser section below.

title: TA5: Introduction to web scraping
text:
Legal and ethical considerations
Scraping is not technically illegal in most jurisdictions, but it can break a contract (the site’s Terms of Service), violate copyright, violate computer-misuse statutes if the target tries to block you and you keep going, or violate privacy law if the data is personal. The rules below are what a careful empirical researcher always follows.

The seven rules of polite scraping

Identify yourself. Always send a meaningful User-Agent header. The script in this TA sends Finance4-TA5-educational-scraper/1.0 (... contact e-mail ...). A site admin who sees that string can decide whether they care; an anonymous bot just gets blocked.
Respect robots.txt. Every well-behaved site publishes one at /robots.txt. Wikipedia’s, for instance, lists every directory that bots are not supposed to crawl. Read it and obey it.
Throttle. A single request per second is a safe default for small projects. The httr2::req_throttle() helper does this for you; the polite-scraping pattern in code/00_utils.R does it via Sys.sleep(1).
Cache everything. Scrape once, analyse forever. The cached HTML is part of your dataset and must be archived alongside your code; otherwise your work is not reproducible.
Honour the licence. Wikipedia is CC-BY-SA: any derivative dataset must keep the attribution and share-alike requirement. SEC EDGAR is public-domain. Most newspapers are not — they ban scraping in their Terms of Service even when their content is public.
Mind GDPR (and equivalent laws). As soon as the data you scrape contains personal information about identifiable EU residents, you are processing personal data and you need a lawful basis under GDPR. “It is on the public web” is not a lawful basis on its own.
Prefer APIs. Wikipedia, GitHub, Twitter (X), the SEC, the Federal Reserve, and most major data sources offer APIs. If an API exists, the API is more stable, more polite, and usually faster. Scraping is the fallback, not the goal.



Examples of where this matters

The hiQ vs LinkedIn litigation in the US (2017–2022) settled the rough principle that scraping public data from a site you do not have an account with is not a Computer Fraud and Abuse Act violation, but the case explicitly did not address ToS or copyright.
The Ryanair vs PR Aviation ruling in the EU (2015) confirmed that a site’s ToS can prohibit scraping even when the scraping itself is technically legal.
Adams & Mosk (2023) scrape letters that constituents send to Congress members — public, government records, free of personal-data issues. That is the kind of source you can scrape safely. A press archive that ToS-prohibits scraping is not.

title: TA5: Introduction to web scraping
section: When you need a real browser
text:
When you need a real browser
Most financial dashboards (Yahoo Finance ticker pages, Bloomberg-like front-ends, fund factsheet viewers, real-time market depth charts) are JavaScript-rendered. The HTML you receive over HTTP is a near-empty shell, and the actual table you can see in the browser is built client-side after the page loads. rvest cannot see that content because rvest does not run JavaScript.
The two standard R answers are:

chromote is a thin wrapper around headless Chrome via the DevTools protocol. It is the modern recommendation: no Java required, well maintained, and easy to install.
RSelenium is the older, classic Selenium binding. More universal — there are Selenium drivers for every browser — but heavier, requires Java, and is best run inside a Docker container.

The pattern with chromote looks like this. We do not run it as part of the TA pipeline because it requires a Chromium install on the lab machine, but the snippet is shipped as output/text/ta5_chromote_snippet.R so you can reuse it in your own work.
library(chromote)
b <- ChromoteSession$new()
b$Page$navigate("https://example.com/dashboard")
b$Page$loadEventFired()
rendered <- b$DOM$getDocument() |>
  purrr::pluck("root", "nodeId") |>
  (\(id) b$DOM$getOuterHTML(nodeId = id))() |>
  purrr::pluck("outerHTML")
b$close()
parsed <- xml2::read_html(rendered)
Once you have parsed, the rest of the pipeline (html_elements, html_table, …) is identical to the static case.

title: TA5: Introduction to web scraping
section: Where this fits in finance research
text:
Where this fits in finance research
Web scraping is rarely the contribution of a paper, but it is often the enabling step without which the paper could not have happened. A few examples directly from the syllabus reading list:

Adams & Mosk (2023) — Dear Congress Member. Scrape letters from constituents to congress members and use NLP to detect mentions of financial regulation. The paper is identification + text analysis; the data only exists because someone wrote a careful scraper.
Akcigit, Baslandze & Lotti (2023) — Connecting to Power. Combine patent data with political-connection records. The political-connection panel is hand-built from a mix of scraped and bulk-downloaded sources.
Babenko, Fedaseyeu & Zhang (2020) — Do CEOs Affect Employees’ Political Choices? Match employee donor records (FEC) to firm rosters. The matching dictionary is built by scraping public profiles.
Akey, Heimer & Lewellen (2021) — Politicising Consumer Credit. Build a panel of FOMC and political appointments by scraping the relevant agency websites.

Notice the pattern: the scrape produces a small, well-documented dataset that is then merged with a standard panel. The paper’s contribution is the merge and the identification — not the scrape — but without the scrape there is no paper.

title: TA5: Introduction to web scraping
text:
Time to code
source("code/00_utils.R")
load_finance4_packages()

REFRESH_CACHE <- FALSE   # set to TRUE if you want a fresh scrape

ensure_dir("data/raw_wikipedia")

The full script is code/06_ta5_webscraping.R. The remainder of this section walks through every result the script produces, in the same order it produces them.

Step 1: Robots.txt and the polite scraper
Before we scrape anything, we fetch and cache /robots.txt. The Wikipedia file is long, so we extract just the directives that matter — User-agent, Disallow, Allow, Crawl-delay. The full log is at output/text/ta5_ethics_log.txt.

Two things to notice:

The header records the exact User-Agent we sent. This is good practice for reproducibility and accountability — anyone reading the log later knows who scraped what and how to contact us.
Wikipedia’s robots.txt is permissive for normal crawlers but explicitly blocks several aggressive bots (MJ12bot, WebCopier, WebStripper, …). Reading the file yourself before you scrape is part of the contract.



Step 2: Scrape the table of largest US banks
The page contains several <table class="wikitable"> blocks. We extract them all with rvest::html_table(fill = TRUE), keep the first one, and clean the column names. Step 4: When static scraping is not enough
Stop-and-think. The script writes out a small chromote snippet to output/text/ta5_chromote_snippet.R that you can lift into your own work later:
# Pseudocode: scraping a JavaScript-rendered page with chromote
library(chromote)
b <- ChromoteSession$new()
b$Page$navigate("https://example.com/dashboard")
b$Page$loadEventFired()
rendered <- b$DOM$getDocument() |>
  purrr::pluck("root", "nodeId") |>
  (\(id) b$DOM$getOuterHTML(nodeId = id))() |>
  purrr::pluck("outerHTML")
b$close()
parsed <- xml2::read_html(rendered)
The point of this snippet is not the lines of code, it is the mental flowchart:

Open the target page in a normal browser.
View the page source. Search for the value you can see on the page.
If the value is in the source, rvest is enough. Stop.
If the value is not in the source, the page is JavaScript-rendered. Use chromote.

We do not run chromote in the TA5 pipeline because installing a Chromium binary on every student’s laptop is a different kind of fight. The snippet is a reference, not a hidden dependency.


Step 5: Tokenisation and term frequencies
Now we turn the lead_text column into one row per word with tidytext::unnest_tokens(), drop standard English stopwords, and remove a small custom list of words that would dominate a corpus made of bank Wikipedia pages (bank, banks, company, incorporated, headquartered, american, …). Customising stopwords is a normal step in finance text analysis: the standard lists are tuned for general English, not for sector-specific corpora.

Step 6: A simple finance tone score
A standard next step in finance text analysis is to score a document with a financial sentiment dictionary — Loughran & McDonald (2011) is the canonical choice. A few things:

The numbers are small — most leads contain only one or two dictionary matches. That is expected from a 600-character excerpt; the absolute score is mainly a function of how the article begins.
The cross-sectional ordering is not random. Banks whose lead emphasises investment, management, successful, innovative score higher; banks whose lead mentions fines, settlements, losses, risk score lower.
Real research scales this up. A typical Loughran-McDonald implementation uses a dictionary of several thousand words and applies it to entire 10-K filings rather than 600-character excerpts.



Step 7: LaTeX exports
The script also exports two LaTeX tables — the bank list and the tone scores — to output/tables/ta5_top_banks.tex and output/tables/ta5_tone_scores.tex, ready to drop into a draft with \input{...}. Together with the cached HTML in data/raw_wikipedia/, this is the full reproducible bundle you would attach to a referee report.


Question for you

Suppose you want to extend this exercise to the largest 100 US banks rather than just the top 15.

Which step (or steps) of the script would you change, and why?
What would you do to keep the scrape polite — both legally and operationally — at that scale?
If the lead paragraphs of the smaller banks turn out to be only one or two sentences, which step of the pipeline would you redesign so that the corpus stays comparable across banks?

title: TA5: Introduction to web scraping
text:
What to take away
A few things to internalise from this TA:

A scraper is a research instrument and should be designed like one: identifiable, throttled, cached, documented, and obedient to the source’s licence and terms.
Static scraping with rvest covers the vast majority of finance use cases. Reach for chromote (or RSelenium) only when the data is genuinely JavaScript-rendered — and check by viewing the page source first.
The cache is the dataset. If your colleague cannot reproduce your tables six months from now without the cached HTML, you have not really done reproducible research.
Once you have the corpus, the rest is small data: tidytext for tokenisation, a transparent dictionary for tone, plain dplyr for everything else. The complicated step is getting the data, not analysing it.

A few questions you should be able to answer:

Why is sending a real User-Agent the cheapest favour you can do for both your project and the server you are scraping?
What is the difference between html_element() and html_elements(), and when does it matter?
Why would you cache scraped HTML on disk even on a fast network connection?
What would it take, operationally and legally, to extend this scrape to a non-Wikipedia source — say, a national newspaper?
Why is the Loughran-McDonald financial sentiment dictionary preferable to a general-purpose dictionary like AFINN or Bing for finance text?

For the formal references behind everything above:

Loughran, T., and B. McDonald (2011), “When is a Liability not a Liability? Textual Analysis, Dictionaries, and 10-Ks”, Journal of Finance 66(1), 35–65.
Adams, R., and T. Mosk (2023), “Dear Congress Member: Letters to Congress and the Shaping of (Financial) Legislation”, working paper, University of Oxford.
Akcigit, U., S. Baslandze, and F. Lotti (2023), “Connecting to Power: Political Connections, Innovation, and Firm Dynamics”, Econometrica 91(2), 529–564.
Babenko, I., V. Fedaseyeu, and S. Zhang (2020), “Do CEOs Affect Employees’ Political Choices?”, Review of Financial Studies 33(4), 1781–1817.
Akey, P., R. Z. Heimer, and S. Lewellen (2021), “Politicizing Consumer Credit”, Journal of Financial Economics 139(2), 627–655.
